comment_text: I watched this movie last night on Netflix and I have to tell you. It was absolutely the dumbest thing I ever watched that is time I will never get back. If you’re going to tell a story about a mass murderer, they keep it on the mass murderer don’t switch back-and-forth between the Texas chainsaw, massacre, or psycho and all the other stuff. I was looking for a much more in-depth factual story about him and about the area that he grew up in. I’ve read the book. It was very good. I had high hopes for this movie. The guy who played the role of Ed endgame I thought he did a hell of a job but the way the movie was put together was a complete total disaster. Somebody must’ve been smoking weed.

comment_text: I watched 4 episodes of this series. Is this a series based on the real life of Ed Gain or not? In this series, he has a girlfriend with whom he unofficially married, a girl who saw Ed's "mother" i.e. what was left of her i.e. what Ed calls mother and at first she was scared but then she was ok with what he was doing. In the 4th episode, Ed has a sexual relationship with a woman who is at least 30 years older than him where Ed is a real stud. In fact, I didn't watch the 4th episode to the end because I don't think the story shown there is real at all. The site where I watch the series has all 8 episodes and to be honest, I thought I would watch them all in a row in 8 hours, like I did with many series but I'm losing interest here. I understand that he was fascinated by what happened in the camps in WW2, that he had a mother who was a hard core Christian, but because of what I wrote about the girlfriend - an unofficial wife and the relationship with an older woman, that's why I ask if the series is based on the real life of Ed Gain, because if it is, then, at least in my opinion, he is misrepresented in most of the documentaries that were made about his life and "work"... Maybe I will continue watching the series, and maybe not, I really have no desire after the "stallion" Ed in action.

comment_text:
Misogyny alert - Modern reinterpretations of the Ed Gein case must recognise that the long-standing attribution of primary blame to his mother, Augusta Gein, reflects the gendered diagnostic culture of the mid-twentieth century rather than a balanced causal analysis. Popular and criminological depictions that portray Augusta as the dominant or near-total (for example, 90%) source of Gein’s pathology reproduce what Phyllis Chesler identified as psychiatry’s “maternal scapegoating” bias, in which female caregivers are pathologised for social or familial dysfunctions originating in male violence and patriarchal constraint (Chesler 1972). In Gein’s family, evidence suggests that his father, George Gein, was an alcoholic and likely abusive man whose violence and failure to provide shaped Augusta’s subsequent isolationism and religious extremism (Schechter 1989). Her behaviour thus emerged not as autonomous cruelty but as a defensive adaptation within a system of coercion—what Judith Herman calls the “repetition of control under trauma” (Herman 1992). To ignore these intersecting conditions while assigning overwhelming causal responsibility to Augusta perpetuates what Jane Ussher terms the “cultural misogyny of madness” (Ussher 1991), in which women’s constrained responses are reinterpreted as evidence of intrinsic pathology. From a twenty-first-century perspective informed by gender studies and trauma theory, such depictions are therefore misogynistic: they erase the initiating male violence and sociostructural inequality that contributed to the same outcomes they attribute to female moral failure.

⸻

Bibliography (Chicago Style)

Caputi, Jane. The Age of Sex Crime. Bowling Green, OH: Bowling Green State University Popular Press, 1987.

Chesler, Phyllis. Women and Madness. New York: Palgrave Macmillan, 1972.

Greven, David. “The Mother of Psychoanalysis: Alfred Hitchcock, Gender, and the Mother.” Film Criticism 37, no. 3 (2013).

Herman, Judith. Trauma and Recovery. New York: Basic Books, 1992.

Schechter, Harold. Deviant: The Shocking True Story of Ed Gein, the Original Psycho. New York: Pocket Books, 1989.

Ussher, Jane M. Women’s Madness: Misogyny or Mental Illness? Amherst: University of Massachusetts Press, 1991.

Walker, Lenore E. The Battered Woman. New York: Harper & Row, 1979.

comment_text:
Straight to the point

A lot of necro, very raunchy lines from these actors/actresses that would make a grown man cringe. It feels like a scrapbook of off ideas thrown together in eight hours. Interesting series nonetheless but I wouldn’t watch it again. Compared to The Dahmer series which I’ve rewatched a few times.

It’s hard for me to believe that people through the 1920s-50s talked the way they did in this doc, and I have no reason to believe they did. I know it’s supposed to connect to this generations “slang and usage of phrase and tone” 

But there’s some very nasty stuff in here that seems like it comes from the mind of a 60 year old perverted man, glamorizing the “fascinations” of this murderer and other characters. Which makes it really hard to watch/believe that all this was going on giving the little information about Ed Gein.

comment_text:
Here's what I found about the voice:

"Hunnam discovered that Gein's voice was an "affectation" or persona developed to please his domineering mother, rather than his authentic self. 

Emulated Gein's persona: According to Hunnam, the tape showed that Gein used his high-pitched, childlike voice to please his mother, who had once wished for a daughter. He adopted this specific vocal performance to illustrate Gein's unhealthy attachment and repressed inner self." 


It seems in his younger years he was a lot more soft spoken. Which makes sense because all he wanted was to please his mother who always wanted a daughter. This could give some insight into the difference we're hearing in the series.
